courses.title: Machine Design II — Machine Elements
courses.summary: The rotating-equipment bill of materials designed properly: gears, rolling and journal bearings, belts and chains, clutches, brakes, and couplings — every element a maintenance engineer has replaced, now sized from first principles.
taught_from: MIT OCW 2.72 Elements of Mechanical Design

lessons.scope: Lewis bending analysis with velocity and geometry factors. Module and face width chosen against tooth breakage. Where root cracks start and why.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: Which two velocity-dependent factors modify Lewis bending stress?
Why do gear root cracks start on the tension side?

lessons.scope: Contact stress by Hertzian logic; pitting as the surface fatigue mode. Bending versus pitting as competing failure budgets. The design meaning of gear micropitting found in an oil sample.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: Bending versus pitting: which failure budget wears out and which breaks?
What does micropitting in an oil report imply about film thickness?

lessons.scope: Helical geometry and axial thrust; bevel basics; worm-drive efficiency and its heat problem. Selection logic across gear types. Thrust-bearing consequences of helix choices.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: Why do helical gears thrust axially, and who pays for it?
Where does a worm drive's efficiency go?

lessons.scope: Bearing types and the L10 life equation used honestly. Equivalent loads with combined radial and thrust. Fits, preload, and mounting — where installed life is won or lost.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: Why does bearing life follow a cube (or 10/3) law in load?
What does preload buy a spindle bearing and cost it?

lessons.scope: Hydrodynamic action from Reynolds-equation reasoning in outline; Sommerfeld thinking and minimum film thickness. Viscosity selection and clearance. The bearings under every large turbine and compressor.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: What builds the oil wedge in a journal bearing?
Why is minimum film thickness the design's real deliverable?

lessons.scope: V-belt mechanics from the capstan relation; power ratings and service factors. Tensioning — the maintenance variable that decides belt life. Multi-belt sets and matching.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: Why do V-belts grip more than flat belts at the same tension?
What does incorrect tension do to belt and bearing life simultaneously?

lessons.scope: Roller-chain geometry and chordal action. Ratings, lubrication regimes, and elongation limits. Sprocket wear read as a diagnostic.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: What is chordal action and what does it do at high speed?
When does chain elongation condemn the sprockets too?

lessons.scope: Friction clutch and brake torque from pressure assumptions — uniform wear versus uniform pressure derived. Energy dissipation and thermal sizing. Where brakes fade and clutches slip.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: Uniform pressure versus uniform wear: which assumption is conservative for clutch torque?
What sizes a brake thermally rather than frictionally?

lessons.scope: Rigid, elastomeric, gear, and disc couplings; what misalignment each tolerates and transmits. Coupling selection against torque and misalignment spectra. The design basis under alignment standards used in the field.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: Match coupling types to the misalignment each forgives.
Why does a rigid coupling turn misalignment into bearing load?

lessons.scope: Rotating-disc stresses and burst margins. Flywheel energy sizing revisited with stress limits. Overspeed as a design case, not an accident report.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: Where is a spinning disc's stress maximum, and why does overspeed test matter?
What margin does a burst test demonstrate?

lessons.t: Design review: a gearbox teardown on paper
lessons.scope: A complete gearbox audited element by element: gears, bearings, shafts, seals, housing. Each element's failure budget identified. The design-side twin of a maintenance teardown report.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design
preview: In the gearbox teardown, which element's failure budget was thinnest?
What would you inspect first at overhaul, and on what design grounds?

courses.title: Mechanical Vibrations
courses.summary: The theory under the analyzer screen: free and forced vibration, damping, multi-degree systems, rotor basics, and isolation — taught so Year 4's condition-monitoring course lands on solid mathematics.

lessons.t: The single degree of freedom system
lessons.scope: Equation of motion from Newton and from energy. Natural frequency's dependence on stiffness and mass — and on nothing else. The model that explains half of all machine vibration behavior.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: Why does natural frequency depend on k and m and nothing else?
Write the SDOF equation of motion from Newton — where does each term come from?

lessons.scope: Viscous damping and the three response regimes. Logarithmic decrement measured from a bump test. Damping ratio as the machine's shock absorber rating.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: Extract damping ratio from successive peak amplitudes — what's the method called?
What separates the three damping regimes physically?

lessons.scope: The frequency response function derived: amplitude and phase versus forcing frequency. Resonance quantified by Q. Why passing through resonance on run-up is survivable but dwelling is not.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: Why does dwelling at resonance destroy what a run-up pass-through survives?
What does Q measure about a resonance?

lessons.scope: Unbalance forcing derived from eccentric mass; response above and below critical speed. The phase flip through resonance. First contact with rotor dynamics language.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: Above its critical speed, where does a rotor's mass center ride, and why is that calmer?
What does the 180° phase flip through a critical tell an analyst?

lessons.scope: Base-excited systems and force transmissibility derived. The isolation region above √2 times natural frequency. Why soft mounts protect and when they betray.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: Why does isolation only begin above √2 times the natural frequency?
When does adding damping worsen isolation?

lessons.scope: Isolator selection: static deflection, natural frequency, damping trade-offs. Inertia blocks and their sizing logic. A compressor installation isolated on paper.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: Why does isolator static deflection encode its natural frequency?
What does an inertia block actually add?

lessons.t: Two degrees of freedom and mode shapes
lessons.scope: Coupled equations, natural frequencies, and mode shapes solved via the eigenproblem promised in Math II. Beating between close modes. The concept structure for all modal analysis.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: What is a mode shape, in one sentence?
Why do two close natural frequencies produce beating?

lessons.t: Multi-DOF and continuous systems in outline
lessons.scope: Modal superposition as the organizing idea. Beam and shaft natural frequencies from standard results. Why long rotors have families of criticals.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: What does modal superposition claim about complex vibration?
Why do long rotors have families of critical speeds?

lessons.scope: Shaft-train torsional models; natural frequencies of two- and three-inertia systems. Excitation from VFDs and reciprocating machines. The invisible vibration that breaks couplings.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: Why is torsional vibration invisible to casing measurements?
What excites torsional modes in VFD-driven trains?

lessons.scope: Accelerometer, velocity, and displacement measurement physics; integration between them. Mounting effects on usable frequency range. The hardware bridge to Year 4's analysis course.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: Why does accelerometer mounting set the usable frequency ceiling?
Integrating acceleration to velocity: what does it amplify?

lessons.scope: Resonance, beats, looseness nonlinearity, and self-excited whirl shown as measured signatures. Each tied back to its lesson's mathematics. The course's theory validated against plant reality.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Mechanical Vibrations; video: Mobius Institute — vibration
preview: Match beats, looseness, and whirl to their measured signatures.
Which lesson's mathematics explains each signature you saw?

courses.title: PLC Fundamentals I
courses.summary: The plant's reflexes: PLC hardware, scan cycle, ladder logic, timers and counters, and disciplined I/O wiring — taught to read-and-troubleshoot level first, author level second.

lessons.scope: Contactors, control relays, and hardwired interlocks. Reading a motor-starter schematic fluently. The relay heritage that ladder logic still speaks.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Read a DOL starter schematic: trace the seal-in path.
What does a control relay isolate?

lessons.t: PLC architecture and the scan cycle
lessons.scope: CPU, I/O, rack, and power supply; the read–execute–write scan and its timing consequences. Why outputs update when they do. Watchdogs and fault states.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Order the scan cycle's three phases and their consequence for fast pulses.
What does the watchdog timer catch?

lessons.scope: Contacts, coils, seal-in circuits, and interlocks — the core patterns of industrial logic, each drawn and traced. Start/stop of a motor with safety interlocks built the standard way. Reading unfamiliar ladder cold: the transition engineer's most bankable automation skill.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: In a seal-in rung, what keeps the motor contactor energized after Start is released — and what breaks it?
Why are stop functions wired through NC contacts: which failure does that choice survive?
Within one scan cycle, at what point do physical outputs actually change state?

lessons.scope: On-delay, off-delay, and retentive timers; up/down counters. Debounce, staging, and timeout patterns. A star-delta starter sequence programmed and traced.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: On-delay versus off-delay: give one use each.
Why do retentive timers survive power cycles — and when is that dangerous?

lessons.scope: Sinking/sourcing, wet and dry contacts, and field-device interfacing. Fusing and commons in the I/O rack. The wiring faults behind most 'PLC problems'.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Wire an NPN sensor to a sinking input: what goes where?
Why do I/O commons trip up mixed-vendor panels?

lessons.scope: 4–20 mA and 0–10 V channels; raw counts to engineering units scaled correctly. Resolution and out-of-range handling. A transmitter read into a displayed value end to end.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Scale 4–20 mA to 0–100 °C: write the two-point formula.
What should logic do with an out-of-range analog value?

lessons.scope: Rungs into routines; tags and comments that survive personnel turnover. State-based thinking for sequences. Programs written to be troubleshot at 3 a.m.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: What makes a program troubleshootable at 3 a.m.?
Why do state machines beat scattered interlocks for sequences?

lessons.scope: Online monitoring, forcing with fear, and cross-referencing. A systematic fault-finding method from symptom to rung to field device. The lesson maintenance hiring managers actually test.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Why is forcing an output dangerous, and what discipline governs it?
From symptom to rung to field device: sketch the fault-finding path.

lessons.t: Safety circuits and the PLC's limits
lessons.scope: E-stops, safety relays, and why safety functions live outside standard PLC logic. Categories of safety architecture in outline. The boundary every integrator must respect.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Why do safety functions live outside the standard PLC?
What does an E-stop circuit guarantee that logic cannot?

lessons.scope: Operator screens: tags, alarms, and navigation that helps at 3 a.m. Alarm philosophy — fewer, truer alarms. The HMI as the machine's public face.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Name three alarm-philosophy rules that keep HMIs useful.
What belongs on a machine's overview screen?

lessons.scope: Program a conveyor start-up with permissives, timers, and fault latching; document it; then troubleshoot three injected faults. The full loop: specify, program, verify. PLC II builds from here.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: In the conveyor project, what fault states did you latch, and why latch at all?
Which permissives gate the start, and in what order?

courses.title: Hydraulics & Pneumatics
courses.summary: Fluid power for people who will maintain it: pumps, valves, actuators, and circuits read from schematics; pressure, flow, and force sized; and the contamination control that decides hydraulic life.
taught_from: NPTEL: Fundamentals of Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: Pascal's law to force multiplication; power as pressure times flow. Where fluid power beats electromechanical drives. The efficiency price paid for force density.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: Why does fluid power win on force density and lose on efficiency?
Compute-style: pressure times flow gives what, in what units?

lessons.scope: Fluid functions and properties; ISO cleanliness codes read and specified. Filtration placement and beta ratios. Contamination as the leading cause of hydraulic failure — treated with due respect.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: Read ISO 4406 18/16/13: what does each number count?
Why does contamination, not wear, head the hydraulic failure list?

lessons.scope: Gear, vane, and piston pumps; fixed versus variable displacement. Volumetric efficiency measured and trended. Pressure-compensated control explained without mystery.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: Gear versus piston pump: efficiency, cost, and cleanliness sensitivity.
What does a pressure-compensated pump hold constant?

lessons.scope: Cylinder sizing for force and speed; cushioning and seal packages. Hydraulic motors in brief. Rod buckling and mounting classes — statics put to work.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: Size a cylinder for force: which area does retraction use?
Why does rod buckling limit long strokes?

lessons.scope: Relief, reducing, and sequence valves; flow controls and meter-in/meter-out logic. Cracking pressure and override characterized. The valves that shape a circuit's personality.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: Relief versus reducing valve: which protects, which regulates?
What does cracking pressure mean for system heat?

lessons.scope: Spool centers and their consequences; ISO 1219 symbols read fluently. Standard circuits: regenerative, counterbalance, unloading. Schematic-to-machine correspondence drilled.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: Read a spool symbol's center condition: what happens de-energized?
Meter-in versus meter-out: which controls an overrunning load?

lessons.scope: Gas-charged accumulators: sizing, precharge, and safety. Shock suppression and supplementary flow. Stored energy respected during maintenance.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: Why does accumulator precharge decide its usable volume?
Name the stored-energy hazard before maintenance.

lessons.scope: Heat, noise, and slow motion as the three complaints; a diagnostic tree for each. Test-point measurements against the schematic. Case-based practice on press and mobile circuits.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: Heat, noise, slow motion: give one cause chain for each complaint.
Why measure at test points instead of trusting gauges on the schematic?

lessons.scope: Air preparation — filter, regulator, lubricator; valve and cylinder families; sizing with compressibility acknowledged. Where pneumatics wins: speed, cleanliness, simplicity. Leakage economics revisited from Thermo II.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: Why does compressibility make pneumatics springy?
What does the FRL unit condition, and in what order?

lessons.scope: Solenoid valves, reed sensors, and PLC-driven pneumatic sequences. Grippers and pick-and-place motions. The actuation layer of packaging machinery.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: Sequence a pick-and-place with two cylinders: what feedback confirms motion?
Why do reed sensors fail, and what replaces them?

lessons.t: Proportional and servo systems first contact
lessons.scope: Proportional valves and closed-loop positioning in outline. Where servo-hydraulics still rules: presses and test rigs. The control-systems course connected to oil.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics
preview: What does a proportional valve add over bang-bang control?
Where does servo-hydraulics still beat electric servos?

courses.title: Welding Engineering & NDT
courses.summary: The joint as an engineered product: weld metallurgy and the HAZ, defects and their acceptance, procedure qualification (WPS/PQR), and the NDT methods — PT, MT, UT, RT — that decide what ships. Kirby and HEISCO territory.
taught_from: Groover, Fundamentals of Modern Manufacturing — joining chapters
Kalpakjian & Schmid, Manufacturing Engineering and Technology

lessons.scope: The weld thermal cycle: peak temperatures and cooling rates across the joint. Fusion zone, HAZ, and base metal as three different materials. Why welding is heat treatment you didn't order.
lessons.src: Groover, welding chapters; Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 10
preview: Sketch the weld thermal cycle: which features control microstructure?
Why is welding 'heat treatment you didn't order'?

lessons.scope: HAZ sub-zones mapped to the Fe–C diagram; hardening, softening, and grain growth located. Carbon equivalent, preheat, and hydrogen control as the cold-cracking triangle. The study guide every welding interview draws from.
lessons.src: Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 9–10; Groover, welding chapters
preview: A carbon equivalent of 0.48 on 25 mm plate triggers which two precautions, and against which cracking mechanism?
Which HAZ sub-zone hardens most, and what two variables set its final hardness?
Why does thicker plate cool a weld faster, and what does that do to martensite risk?

lessons.scope: Why welds pull: thermal contraction against restraint. Distortion modes and sequencing countermeasures. PWHT — what it relieves and what it costs.
lessons.src: Kalpakjian & Schmid, joining chapters
preview: Why do welds pull, and which direction does a butt joint shrink most?
What does PWHT relieve, and what does it cost?

lessons.scope: Porosity, lack of fusion, undercut, cracks hot and cold, inclusions — each with its cause chain and its radiographic/ultrasonic signature. Discontinuity versus defect: acceptance criteria as the deciding line. WPS/PQR logic: qualifying a procedure, then policing it.
lessons.src: Groover, welding chapters; Kalpakjian & Schmid
preview: On a radiograph, how does lack of fusion look different from porosity?
Discontinuity versus defect: what single thing turns the first into the second?
WPS versus PQR: which is the tested evidence, which the working recipe, and in what order do they exist?

lessons.t: Welding symbols and joint design review
lessons.scope: Symbol fluency on fabrication drawings; effective throat and size selection recalled from design. Access and position as quality factors. Reading a Kirby-style shop drawing's weld map.
lessons.src: Groover; Kalpakjian & Schmid
preview: Read effective throat from a fillet symbol: what fails if undersized?
Why does access decide weld quality before the arc strikes?

lessons.scope: Carbon and low-alloy steels, stainless (sensitization and ferrite control), and aluminum's oxide and porosity problems. Dissimilar joints in outline. Matching filler to base with reasons.
lessons.src: Kalpakjian & Schmid; Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 11
preview: Why does austenitic stainless demand ferrite control in the weld?
What makes aluminum's oxide layer a welding problem?

lessons.t: NDT principles and the methods matrix
lessons.scope: PT, MT, UT, and RT: physical principle, what each detects, surface versus volumetric reach, and access/geometry limits — condensed into the selection matrix inspectors actually use. Personnel qualification levels demystified. Which method finds which weld defect, and why.
lessons.src: Groover, inspection chapters; Kalpakjian & Schmid
preview: A surface-breaking crack in painted ferromagnetic steel: PT or MT, and why?
Why can RT miss a tight planar crack that UT finds easily?
Pick methods: subsurface porosity in a 12 mm aluminum weld versus a surface fatigue crack on a shaft.

lessons.t: Volumetric NDT in depth: UT and RT
lessons.scope: Ultrasonic testing: wave propagation, probes, calibration blocks, and angle-beam weld scanning — sizing and characterizing indications, where UT skill actually lives. Radiographic testing: source selection, geometry, image quality indicators, and film and digital reading of the classic defect appearances. Radiation safety as an absolute boundary.
lessons.src: Kalpakjian & Schmid, inspection sections
preview: Why does angle-beam UT find what straight-beam misses in welds?
What does an IQI (penetrameter) prove about a radiograph?

lessons.scope: WPS variables — essential and nonessential; PQR testing: tensiles, bends, macros. Welder performance qualification and its limits of position and thickness. The paperwork that makes welds trustworthy.
lessons.src: Groover; Kalpakjian & Schmid
preview: Essential versus nonessential WPS variables: what changes force requalification?
What do bend tests reveal that tensiles miss?

lessons.scope: Arc gouging and defect excavation; repair sequences that don't make things worse. Excavation verification by MT/PT. Repair welding as controlled surgery, permitted and documented.
lessons.src: Kalpakjian & Schmid
preview: Why verify excavation by MT/PT before rewelding?
What documentation must a repair weld leave behind?

lessons.scope: Assemble an inspection and test plan for a welded assembly: hold points, NDT extents, acceptance criteria, and records. The QA/QC document flow of a fabrication contract walked once end to end.
lessons.src: Kalpakjian & Schmid; Groover
preview: What belongs at a hold point versus a witness point?
Why does an ITP bind subcontractors before work starts?

courses.title: Industrial Safety
courses.summary: Staying alive around energy: hazard recognition, permits and isolation, machine guarding, hot work, confined spaces, and the incident lessons the Gulf's process industry paid for in blood.

lessons.t: How people get hurt in plants
lessons.scope: Energy-based hazard thinking: mechanical, electrical, pressure, thermal, chemical, gravity. Incident pyramids and their honest critique. Safety as engineering, not posters.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: Name the energy families and one hazard each contributes.
Why do incident pyramids mislead as prediction tools?

lessons.scope: Hazard identification, likelihood–consequence matrices, and the hierarchy of controls. Job safety analysis written for a real maintenance task. Residual risk stated, not hidden.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: Order the hierarchy of controls and defend why PPE is last.
What makes a JSA suitable and sufficient?

lessons.scope: Isolation points, stored energy, and verification of zero energy. Group lockout on complex equipment. The procedure that most directly keeps maintenance engineers alive.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: What does verification of zero energy add to isolation?
Group lockout: who holds what?

lessons.scope: Permit types — general, hot work, confined space, line break — and their control logic. Permit interfaces between operations and maintenance. Why permits fail: signatures without verification.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: Why do permits fail — name two systemic causes.
What triggers a line-break permit over a general one?

lessons.scope: Point-of-operation, nip points, and rotating-shaft hazards. Guard types and interlocked access. The guard-defeat culture problem addressed as engineering.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: Why are interlocked guards defeated, and what design response works?
Identify three nip points on a belt conveyor.

lessons.scope: Definition, atmospheres, and gas testing. Entry roles and rescue planning that isn't fictional. Tanks, pits, and vessels — where multiple-fatality events happen.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: What three atmospheric tests precede confined-space entry?
Why must rescue plans be non-fictional — what makes them real?

lessons.scope: Hot-work hazards from welding lecture context; fire triangle and watch requirements. Flammable atmospheres and gas freeing. Fabrication-yard discipline as practiced at scale.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: What does a fire watch actually watch for, and for how long after?
Why does hot work near tanks demand gas testing over time?

lessons.scope: Fall protection systems; scaffolding basics; crane and rigging fundamentals with load charts respected. Dropped-object prevention. The physics of statics applied to staying alive.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: What does a load chart's radius column do to capacity?
Name the anchor-point rule for fall arrest.

lessons.t: Personal protective equipment and its limits
lessons.scope: PPE as the last line in the hierarchy of controls, never the first answer. Head, eye, hearing, respiratory, hand, and foot protection matched to assessed hazards; fit, compatibility, and maintenance as the quiet failure points. Why PPE programs decay silently — and what keeps them honest.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: Why does PPE sit last in the hierarchy of controls?
Name two ways a respirator program fails without anyone noticing.

lessons.scope: Heat illness physiology and the WBGT logic of work-rest cycles. Summer work rules in Kuwait's climate. Hydration and acclimatization as engineering controls, not advice.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: Why is WBGT better than air temperature for Gulf work-rest cycles?
What acclimatization does and doesn't protect against?

lessons.scope: Evidence preservation, timelines, and causal analysis without blame-first shortcuts. Corrective actions that change systems, not just people. The investigation skill reused in reliability RCA.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering
preview: Why does blame-first investigation destroy the evidence you need?
What distinguishes a corrective action from a countermeasure that survives?
